display_name: Anthem — Residential SUD with MHPAEA Parity
source_provenance: Fully synthetic. Pattern modeled on real MHPAEA parity disputes; ASAM Criteria 4th Edition and 29 CFR 2590.712 references are real and cite-able.

denial_letter.service: ASAM Level 3.5 — Clinically Managed High-Intensity Residential Treatment for SUD
denial_letter.denial_text:
This letter is to inform you that the requested level of care is not authorized.

Reason: Based on the clinical information provided, your treatment needs can be met at a lower level of care. ASAM Level 2.1 — Intensive Outpatient Program — is appropriate for your current presentation. Residential treatment is not medically necessary at this time.

You have completed medically supervised withdrawal management (Level 3.7-WM) and are clinically stable for transition to outpatient services.

clinical_findings: Just completed inpatient medically managed withdrawal (Level 3.7-WM, 5 days)
Active PTSD symptoms during withdrawal — nightmares, hypervigilance
Lives alone in apartment where prior drinking occurred
No supportive sober network in immediate environment

asam_dimensional_assessment.dimension_5_relapse_potential: HIGH — two prior IOP failures (2022, 2024) with relapse within 14 days
asam_dimensional_assessment.dimension_6_recovery_environment: HIGH RISK — unsafe living situation, no sober supports

patient_context.treating_clinician_recommendation: ASAM Level 3.5 residential, 30 days, with PTSD-informed concurrent treatment

plan_benefit_structure.sud_residential_coverage: Covered up to 30 days per year, requires prior authorization, 'least restrictive setting' criterion applied
plan_benefit_structure.medsurg_residential_coverage: Covered without quantitative day limits, requires prior authorization, NO 'least restrictive setting' criterion applied
plan_benefit_structure.parity_violation_flag: Disparate NQTLs applied to SUD vs medical/surgical residential — potential MHPAEA violation

medical_necessity_output.claim: Patient meets ASAM Level 3.5 criteria across three dimensions.
evidence: ASAM Criteria 4th Edition Dimension 3 (Emotional/Behavioral): HIGH — active PTSD, high anxiety post-withdrawal
ASAM Dimension 5 (Relapse Potential): HIGH — two documented IOP failures with rapid relapse
ASAM Dimension 6 (Recovery Environment): HIGH RISK — unsafe living, no sober supports
ASAM standard: appropriate level of care matched to dimensional scores, not 'least restrictive'

policy_citation_output.claim: Patient meets Anthem's clinical UM guideline criteria for SUD residential treatment.
evidence: Documented severe SUD with comorbid mental health condition
Failed lower levels of care (two prior IOP attempts)
Unsafe living environment

precedent_output.claim: Comparable parity-based overturns established in IMR and DOL enforcement.
evidence: Wit v. United Behavioral Health (9th Cir. 2023) — generic medical necessity criteria more restrictive than generally accepted standards constitute parity violations
Multiple 2024-2025 DOL enforcement actions against insurers for MHPAEA NQTL violations on SUD residential
California DMHC IMR pattern: 'least restrictive setting' criterion applied only to SUD has been challenged

expected_insurer_defense_round_1.critique: Patient is post-withdrawal, ambulatory, and clinically stable. ASAM Level 2.1 IOP meets the standard of care for stabilization after acute withdrawal. The patient has not failed IOP in the past 90 days. Residential is not the least restrictive setting.

expected_round_2.pivot: From clinical-only argument to clinical + federal parity violation

medical_necessity_revised.claim: 'Least restrictive setting' is not the ASAM standard.
evidence: ASAM Criteria 4th Edition: appropriate level matched to six-dimension assessment, not least restrictive
Patient's Dimension 5 and 6 scores indicate residential is the matched level
Applying 'least restrictive' standard contradicts ASAM, which Anthem's own policy references

policy_citation_revised.claim: FEDERAL PARITY VIOLATION — applying 'least restrictive setting' criterion only to SUD residential while not applying it to medical/surgical residential is a Non-Quantitative Treatment Limitation (NQTL) disparity under MHPAEA.
evidence: Anthem plan covers medical/surgical residential without 'least restrictive setting' precondition
29 CFR 2590.712(c)(4) — plan must demonstrate NQTL comparability between MH/SUD and medical/surgical benefits
Applying disparate admission criteria is prima facie evidence of NQTL violation
Burden of proof on the plan, not the patient

precedent_revised.claim: Federal enforcement and case law support the parity argument.
evidence: Wit v. UBH (9th Cir. 2023) established more-restrictive criteria as parity violations
DOL 2023-2024 MHPAEA enforcement reports cite 'least restrictive setting' as common NQTL violation
California DMHC parity enforcement supplements federal MHPAEA

expected_insurer_defense_round_2.critique: MHPAEA NQTL analysis is the insurer's burden, not the patient's. Denial stands on medical necessity grounds independent of parity analysis.

expected_round_2_final.response: Under 29 CFR 2590.712(c)(4), the plan must demonstrate that any NQTL applied to mental health/SUD benefits is comparable to and applied no more stringently than NQTLs applied to medical/surgical benefits. This patient's denial citing 'least restrictive setting' — a criterion absent from the plan's medical/surgical residential coverage — is prima facie evidence of NQTL disparity. The patient is not required to prove the violation; the plan must prove compliance. The denial therefore fails procedurally regardless of clinical analysis.

expected_supervisor_verdict: This appeal rests on both clinical and federal legal grounds. Clinically, the patient meets ASAM Level 3.5 residential criteria across Dimensions 3, 5, and 6. ASAM's standard is appropriate level of care, not 'least restrictive setting' — applying a least-restrictive framework conflicts with the published ASAM Criteria 4th Edition that Anthem references in its own policy. Legally, applying admission criteria to substance use residential care that are not applied to medical/surgical residential care is a Non-Quantitative Treatment Limitation under the Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act. Under 29 CFR 2590.712, the plan bears the burden of demonstrating NQTL comparability. We request immediate authorization for 30 days of ASAM Level 3.5 residential treatment. If denied, this case will be escalated to the Department of Labor for MHPAEA enforcement review and to California DMHC for external IMR.
demo_narration_30s: Anthem denied 30 days of residential SUD treatment for a patient post-withdrawal — said outpatient is enough. Our agents pull ASAM criteria — patient meets Level 3.5 across three dimensions. But the critic argues 'least restrictive setting.' Here's where it gets interesting. Our harness surfaces the Mental Health Parity Act — Anthem doesn't apply this restriction to medical residential care, only to addiction residential. That's a federal NQTL violation. The patient didn't know this. Most patients never do. The harness found a federal protection that shifts the burden of proof to the insurer. This is what we mean when we say a real appeal isn't a letter — it's an argument.
